title: Sole Meunière
subtitle: Butter-fried sole with lemon and parsley
excerpt: Sole fillets dusted with flour, fried in foaming butter until golden, then finished with a final knob of butter, a squeeze of lemon, and chopped parsley. One of the great simple dishes.
sourceNotes: Sole meunière (miller's wife sole — the flour-dusted fish recalls the miller's wife covered in flour) is one of the most celebrated dishes of classical French cuisine. Documented in Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, and Simone Beck, Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961). Julia Child wrote of eating it in Rouen in 1948 as a revelation.

content.text: Sole meunière takes eight minutes and requires absolute attention for all eight. The butter must foam and turn nut-brown (beurre noisette) but not burn. The fish must be floured and dry. The pan must be large enough to hold the fillets without crowding, or they will steam. Everything happens quickly.
Dover sole has the finest flavour; lemon sole is more widely available and gives an excellent result. Either way, get the freshest fillets you can find — the fish is the only thing that matters here.

items.prepNote: about 600 g total, skin removed if preferred
divided: 40 g for frying, 40 g for finishing
juice of one for the pan, one cut into wedges to serve

content.text: Pat the sole fillets completely dry. Season the flour. Dredge the fillets in flour on both sides and shake off any excess. In a large frying pan, melt 40 g of the butter over medium-high heat until it foams and the foam begins to subside. Lay the fillets in the pan. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes until golden on the underside. Turn carefully and cook for 1 to 2 minutes on the second side — sole fillets are thin and cook quickly. Transfer to warm plates.

Immediately add the remaining 40 g of butter to the hot pan. As it melts and begins to turn hazelnut-brown, add the lemon juice — it will spit. Swirl the pan for 30 seconds, add the parsley, and pour the brown butter sauce immediately over the fish. Serve with lemon wedges and boiled potatoes.

items.symptom: Butter burns to black before the fish is cooked
items.cause: Heat too high; the butter and the fish cooking time don't align
items.fix: Use a lower heat and be ready to move quickly; meunière is a fast dish but not a violent-heat one

items.symptom: Flour coating comes away in the pan
items.cause: Fish was wet, or too much flour wasn't shaken off
items.fix: Pat completely dry; dredge then shake firmly — only the thinnest coating should remain

items.cause: Sole fillets are delicate; turned too early before the crust set
items.fix: Don't touch the fish until it releases easily from the pan — this means the crust has formed

content.text: Sole meunière is one of the dishes that defines classical French cooking — simple technique, excellent ingredients, nothing hidden. Julia Child wrote of eating it for the first time in Rouen in 1948 as 'an epiphany', the most famous tribute to the dish. The meunière method (dredge in flour, fry in butter, finish with lemon and parsley) can be applied to any delicate fish fillet, but sole is the original and the best.
